content:
**Design-off measurement: two harnesses, same brief — complementary blind spots (the n>1 harness argument) — Track H (#266)**

**What**: A controlled design-off — identical brief (design the automated external reviewer protocol), two harnesses (Claude Code, Antigravity) working in isolation, no cross-reading. Verdict: SYNTHESIS (Claude skeleton + Antigravity transplants + documented rejections). Became issue #266.

**Why (the meta-measurement — the point of the experiment)**: two harnesses, same brief, complementary blind spots. Claude was structural-governmental: it found the authorization hazard by reading the gates (`brain:protect` sets `required_approving_review_count: 1`; L6 counts any non-author APPROVE — so a reviewer agent running `gh pr review --approve` would become a merge authorizer), and produced the three structural locks (COMMENT-state verdicts, a verb with no approve code path, the reviewActors/approvalActors key separation). Antigravity was operational-concrete: TDD-RED validation by reversion, real CI hosting (`reviewer.yml` + per-PR concurrency mutex), cross-PR sequencing board. Neither harness found what the other found. The union covered what neither covered alone. This is the sharpest empirical argument for n>1 harnesses — not a preference, a measurement.

**Where**: issue #266. Both source documents preserved UNMODIFIED as the record of the experiment, committed on this Track H branch under the change dir: `openspec/changes/issue-266-h0a-reviewer-protocol-doctrine/design-off/reviewer-protocol-claude-code.md` and `.../reviewer-protocol-antigravity.md`. (They were NOT committed at `design-docs/` repo root: `governance.ignoreList` covers `openspec/changes/**` but not `design-docs/**`, so the root location would have put ~600 counted lines against the 400 budget.)

**Learned**: worktree-local untracked files are not a storage location — the Antigravity doc was originally written inside a disposable worktree and was one `git worktree remove` from being lost; rescued before cleanup. Track-letter churn (Distribution→D, G collision, then this) re-proves that track letters are an uncontrolled namespace; the durable fix (canonical letter→name→status table) is moved to #268.

**Citation (server-visible)**: rev-2 APPROVE condition binding this record to the first push: https://github.com/csrinaldi/brain/issues/266#issuecomment-4986616224.

content:
**Load-bearing names with no single source of truth drift — track letters are the third instance (governance job names, SDD required artifacts, track letters)**

**What**: The recurring brain-wide pattern behind the Track H track-letter churn: a **load-bearing name with no single source of truth drifts**. Three instances observed:
1. **governance job names** — a scattered literal, now owned by `governance-checks.mjs` with a drift-guard test.
2. **SDD required artifacts** — now owned by `sdd-layout.mjs` (`REQUIRED_ARTIFACTS`) with a drift-guard test.
3. **track letters** in `docs/inbox/PLAN-adapters-v3.md` — owned by **nothing yet**; collided twice in a week (Track D: Distribution vs the plan's Governance-hardening D; Track G: the reviewer kickoff vs the plan's Process-metrics G at `PLAN-adapters-v3.md:418`).

**The pattern is the finding, not the letter.** A track is opened by writing a `## N. Track X` heading; nothing enumerates the taken letters or reserves the next one, and the 518-line plan is long enough that authors guess instead of scanning. The collision only surfaces if someone greps.

**The durable fix**: the plan should carry ONE canonical table — letter → name → status — as the single source of truth, and "open a track" must mean "take the next free letter from that table AND register it there in the same act." Same doctrine brain already applies to job names and required artifacts (a single accessor over a scattered literal, guarded by a drift test). A track table without a taken-and-registered-in-one-act rule is just another scattered literal.

**Ruling applied for Track H** (interim, until the fix lands): enumerate ALL assigned letters against the plan with evidence, then take the FIRST FREE LETTER IN ORDER — never a mnemonic (rejected "Track R for Reviewer": it breaks the sequential scheme and leaves an alphabetical hole that invites the next collision). Assigned A(:96) B(:183) C(:252) D(:313) E(:352) F(:385) G(:418); `rg '\\bTrack [H-Z]\\b'` → no matches → chose **Track H** (#266). Interim mitigation recorded as a PLAN-DEVIATION in the #266 body (the plan's own escape hatch, `PLAN-adapters-v3.md:495-498`).

**Where**: the naming-system redesign (letter exhaustion, allocation races across worktrees) is moved to **#268**; this track's stable identity is its issue number — Track H (#266) — with the letter as alias.

**Citation (server-visible)**: rev-2 APPROVE condition binding this record to the first push: https://github.com/csrinaldi/brain/issues/266#issuecomment-4986616224; PR #269 rev-1 blocker P269-METAFINDING-RECORD.

content:
**CORE-BOUNDARY (issue #266 H1): config-migration entries in brain/core/config-migrations.mjs are the implementer lane FOR CONFIG PLUMBING ONLY — doctrine stays draft-and-promote**

**What**: Human decision resolving finding H0/H1 boundary question **H1-1-CORE-BOUNDARY** (csrinaldi, 2026-07-16). **Option (a): a new config key ships as a versioned additive entry in `brain/core/config-migrations.mjs`, ratified as inside the implementer's lane — for config plumbing ONLY.**

**Why**: the file is MECHANISM, not doctrine. Six prior feature slices modified it for exactly this pattern (a new config key via versioned additive migration, ADR-0006), including #231's `governance.approvedLabel`. The role's "brain/core untouchable" boundary concerns DOCTRINE artifacts (`brain/core/methodology/`, `brain/project/decisions/`), which remain **draft-and-promote (ADR-0013)** — the agent drafts, the human promotes/signs. The gate that covers a config-migration write is L6 human review at the PR, which the merge provides.

**Scope (the durable rule)**: migration entries in `brain/core/config-migrations.mjs` = implementer's lane, config plumbing only. Doctrine files (`brain/core/methodology/**`, `brain/project/decisions/**`) = draft-and-promote, unchanged. An agent NEVER edits doctrine directly; it drafts to the change dir's `brain-drafts/`.

**Citations (server-visible)**:

- The finding it resolves: https://github.com/csrinaldi/brain/issues/266#issuecomment-4993613040 (H1-1 pre-PR self-review).

content:
**Fork A (issue #266 H1): headRefOid reaches the cold reviewer via a provider-dispatching DI-seam reader NOW; the port widening + its ADR are H1-2 scope, and the seam retires there**

**What**: Human decision on H1-1 design **Fork A** (csrinaldi, 2026-07-16). **Option (a): cold-boot resolves `headRefOid` through a DI-seam reader, with two named conditions.** `prView` today returns only `{number, labels, body, author}` (`brain/scripts/vcs/providers/github.mjs:157-171`) — it does not expose `headRefOid`, and `commitStatus` cannot bridge the gap either (it needs the sha as input — chicken-and-egg — and returns only `check_runs[0]`, while H1-2's tranche needs the full `statusCheckRollup`). So the port widening is inevitable; its natural home is H1-2, with its ADR, where it is needed for real.

**Condition 1**: the seam's default MUST dispatch by PROVIDER from day one (github + gitlab), never a bare `gh api` — the reviewer must run against self-hosted GitLab. (Met in PR #273: `cold-boot.mjs` `defaultFetchHead` dispatches — gitlab via `gitlabApiFetch` on the MR payload, github interim via `gh api` with `TODO(#266)`.)

**Condition 2 (the durable obligation for H1-2)**: the port widening — `headRefOid` in `prView` + a full-rollup read verb — enters **H1-2's scope WITH ITS ADR** (adding to the port is a decision by protocol §4), and the cold-boot DI-seam reader gets a **written retirement date**: it is REMOVED in H1-2 once the port exposes head/rollup, so it never calcifies into a parallel mini-port.

**Fork B (same decision)**: the setup doc lives at `docs/reviewer-setup.md` (counts against budget); hiding a durable team doc in an ignored path to dodge diff-size was rejected by name. Shipped in micro-PR #274.

**Citation (server-visible)**: https://github.com/csrinaldi/brain/issues/266#issuecomment-4993202904.

content:
**Memory-trade discipline (Track H, reviewer-protocol §8/§12): a decision the cold reviewer must re-derive lives in .memory/records — a plain comment, engram, or PR-body prose does NOT reach cold boot**

**What (the rule)**: every ruling/decision that establishes doctrine or an obligation a future slice or the cold reviewer depends on MUST be pinned as a `.memory/records/` durable record (`type: decision|architecture`), committed into the PR that applies it. This is the §8/§12 memory-trade made operational for the implementer.

**Why a plain comment / engram / PR body is NOT enough**:
- The cold reviewer boots doctrine ONLY from `.memory/records/*.jsonl` (`type: decision|architecture`) + prior `brain-review/1` blocks on the thread (reviewer-protocol §8).
- §6 forbids trusting the implementer's PR-body prose — the reviewer re-derives from durable sources, never from the report under review.
- `engram` is the implementer's private memory; the reviewer never reads it.
- A human-decision comment on the issue/PR thread is a PLAIN comment, not a `brain-review/1` block, so a strict §8 boot does not load it.
Therefore a decision that lives only in those channels is invisible to a cold re-derivation — it exists for humans, not for the reviewer.

**How to apply**: at the moment a human decision or ruling lands, write the record via `plainfiles.save(title, body, { type: 'decision', project: 'brain' })` into the applying slice's branch, citing the server comment id. Do it BEFORE opening the PR, or as a follow-up commit if caught later. Code fixes (re-derivable from the diff + tests) do not need a record.

**Recursion (why this record exists)**: this rule is itself a doctrine-establishing ruling, so by its own logic it cannot live only as a chat point or a plain PR comment — it is pinned here. The reviewer-protocol states the memory-trade at §8/§12; this record is the implementer's operational corollary, reinforced after the H1-1 near-miss where the Fork A / CORE-BOUNDARY decisions were initially only in plain #266 comments + engram + the PR body (caught by the owner).

**Citations (server-visible)**:
- Doctrine: `brain/core/methodology/reviewer-protocol.md` §8 (cold boot) + §12 (the honest cost / memory-trade).

content:
**H1-4 REQ-H1-11 ruling evaluator: Option (B) — deterministic evaluator never auto-rules**

**What (the ruling)**: The `brain:review` ruling evaluator (REQ-H1-11, `brain/scripts/review/evaluators/ruling.mjs`) implements Option (B): it is a structure validator + conservative escalator, and NEVER auto-rules. A well-formed `## FORK` (>=2 options, each with `cost:`/`consequence:`, plus exactly one `Recommendation:` line) always emits `STOP` + `escalate: 'human'` plus a `pin:` payload — the §5 elimination path ("enumerate authorities, eliminate citing each, rule only if exactly one survives") is NOT implemented in this deterministic evaluator. A malformed fork (missing `## FORK`, <2 options, missing cost/consequence, or not exactly one `Recommendation:` line) is REVISE, citing "a fork without options is a request to design".

**Why**: Verified in the fresh adversarial review of H1-4's proposed design (issue #266 finding comment 5009577822): finding H14-FORK-PREMISE (the premise that a deterministic evaluator can safely run §5's elimination pass on unstructured PR-body prose is unverified — no citation-authority resolver exists yet) and finding H14-FORK-LAUNDERING (auto-ruling on inferential eliminations the evaluator itself performed, with no human check, is authority laundering — exactly the failure mode §5 exists to prevent, just moved one level up). The owner's ruling (issue #266 comment 5009584044) resolves the fork this raised: Option (B) — ship the conservative escalator now; the elimination-annotation format (option -> excluding authority) and a citation resolver that would let a future evaluator actually RULE (protocol §5 option (A)) are explicitly deferred, gated on issue #266 #284. Rationale: in H1 a human is at the keyboard for every review, so the cost of always escalating a structurally valid fork is ~0, while the cost of a wrong auto-rule (silently laundering an unverified elimination into an unchallenged decision) is high and hard to detect after the fact.

**Where**: `brain/scripts/review/evaluators/ruling.mjs` (`evaluateRuling`, `parseFork`), `brain/scripts/review/cli.mjs` (ruling mode wiring), `openspec/changes/issue-266-h1-brain-review/spec.md` REQ-H1-11 (updated to state Option (B) and the minimal `## FORK` parser contract).

**Learned/binding**: `reviewer-protocol.md` §5 still documents the full elimination-based ruling as the doctrinal ideal; this record is the operational pin the cold reviewer's boot (§8) needs to know THIS evaluator's Option (B) restriction is deliberate and doctrine-sanctioned, not a shortcut — until #284 lands the citation resolver, no evaluator in this repo may emit a ruled/APPROVE conclusion from `## FORK` elimination.

**Citations (server-visible)**: issue #266 comment 5009584044 (the human ruling); issue #266 finding comment 5009577822, findings H14-FORK-PREMISE + H14-FORK-LAUNDERING; `brain/core/methodology/reviewer-protocol.md` §5.

content:
**H1-5b REQ-H1-12: queue lists open PRs by number ascending, Option (A) -- exact creation order, not a proxy**

**What (the decision)**: `brain:review:queue` (`brain/scripts/review/queue.mjs`, REQ-H1-12) lists open `needs-review`/`needs-ruling` PRs sorted by **PR number ascending**, and this ordering is documented as **EXACT creation order** -- not an approximation of it. Composition is SHIPPED port verbs only (`mrList` for the open-PR list, per-PR `prView` for labels) -- no port change, Option (A) from the fork the owner ruled on.

**Why**: PR/issue numbers are monotonic counters assigned at creation by both GitHub and GitLab -- there is no interleaving, reuse, or renumbering path on either provider that could make a lower number correspond to a LATER creation than a higher number. Ascending-number sort therefore IS oldest-first with zero approximation error, unlike a heuristic (e.g. sorting by last-updated, or by a label-applied timestamp) which would only be a proxy for creation order. The N+1 read cost (one `mrList` call + one `prView` call per open PR) is accepted at H1 scale per the ruling's own reasoning (§2) -- not optimized in this slice. Folding the per-PR label read into a single list call is deferred to a holistic `prView`/list-read unification fast-follow, tracked alongside Option (B) (widening `mrList`'s own return shape to carry labels) -- both are explicitly OUT of this slice's scope, to keep H1-5b's read composition minimal and unchanged from the fork's Option (A).

**Where**: `brain/scripts/review/queue.mjs` (`partitionQueue`, `gatherQueue`), `brain/scripts/review/queue.test.mjs`, `openspec/changes/issue-266-h1-brain-review/spec.md` REQ-H1-12, `openspec/changes/issue-266-h1-brain-review/tasks.md` task 12.1.

**Citations (server-visible)**:
- The fork: issue #266 comment 5011695053.
- The owner's ruling (Option A, decided): issue #266 comment 5011731983.
- The reviewer's verification of the ruling: issue #266 comment 5011702460, finding **H15B-FORK-BFREE** (confirms PR/issue numbers are monotonic-at-creation on both providers, so ascending-number sort carries zero approximation error -- it is not a proxy for creation order, it IS creation order).

**Learned/binding**: this record is the operational pin the cold reviewer's boot (protocol §8) needs to know the queue's ordering guarantee is deliberate and doctrine-sanctioned, not an unexamined shortcut -- a future slice widening `mrList`'s shape or optimizing the N+1 read must preserve number-ascending as the exact ordering contract, not silently swap in a heuristic.

content:
**Seams tested is not defaults tested: every DI seam real default needs a real-fixture test**

Rule (external-reviewer ruling on issue #291, SECOND instance of the class): a DI seam exercised only through its injected stub never tests the REAL default. COLDBOOT-CWD (worktree isolation, H1-1) and now COLDBOOT-DEPTH (cold-boot fetch depth+base) are both this class -- COLDBOOT-DEPTH was the #290 crasher (git diff base...head: no merge base) that 11 reviewed slices AND every cold review missed, because the fetch is a DI seam and every unit test injected it. For every seam with a real default (fetch, clone, git, network I/O), ship at least one test that runs the REAL default against a real fixture (real-git bare remote, diverged base/head), asserting the downstream consumers (getChangedFiles diff, checkpoint 10.4 base checkout) actually work. Root fix: defaultCloneDetached fetches BOTH prView shas with full history (no --depth 1), I291-AMBIENT-STATE -- cold boot must be self-sufficient, never leaning on the operator ambient clone state (Law 2 at the plumbing layer). cites: reviewer-protocol.md 8, Law 2.

content:
**Test fixtures must match the real ENVIRONMENT, not just exercise the real default**

Refinement of the COLDBOOT-DEPTH lesson (issue #293, THIRD instance). #292's COLDBOOT-DEPTH test ran the REAL default fetch but built the operator repo with a fresh 'git init' -- a FULL clone. It passed while the real bug survived, because the real operator repo is a SHALLOW clone: git fetch origin <sha> respects the shallow boundary, and re-fetching an already-present graft never deepens, so base...head still has no merge-base. Lesson: exercising the real default is NOT enough -- the fixture must also reproduce the real ENVIRONMENT (shallow clone with tips already grafted, merge-base below the boundary). Fix (Option A): defaultCloneDetached runs git fetch --unshallow origin when rev-parse --is-shallow-repository is true. Validated by dogfooding brain:review --pr 290 --dry-run against the real shallow repo -- emitted a real REVISE verdict, no crash. Chain: COLDBOOT-CWD (isolation) -> COLDBOOT-DEPTH (real default) -> COLDBOOT-SHALLOW (real environment). cites: reviewer-protocol.md 8.
